prompt:
The Elan design system case study reads like a feature list. It shows
what the token naming convention is but not why it was chosen. There
is no comparison to IBM Carbon or Goldman Sachs. The section hierarchy
has "Token Architecture" under "Lumen Color Identity" when it should
be the other way around. This is a showcase, not a rationale. Fix the
content strategy.
response: I now have complete context across all dimensions. Here is the full diagnosis. --- ## Diagnosis You have **three compounding problems** on the Élan case study, each a distinct failure mode, all present simultaneously. The feedback is a regression — the knowledge graph shows CFB-014 and CFB-019 already documented these exact issues being "fixed" in earlier sessions before the v3 rebuild, meaning the rebuild reintroduced them. --- ## Root Cause ### Problem 1 — Feature-List Narrative (CAP-016) **Anti-pattern: CAP-016 — Feature-List Case Study (Showing WHAT Without WHY)** The token naming convention is described as a *feature* of the system ("here is the convention: `color/action/primary`") rather than as a *decision* with a rationale chain. The reader is shown the output but not the thinking. A hiring manager at IBM Carbon or Goldman Sachs Design Systems reads "Lumen uses a semantic naming scheme" and thinks: *so does every design system built after 2019*. Without a WHY — "We considered Carbon's role-based scheme and Figma Tokens' depth-first hierarchy, and chose semantic flattening because our AI agent can't navigate three-tier paths reliably" — the convention is just inventory. The Decisions beat (Beat 5 in the Narrative Arc) is the highest-value signal for seniority evaluation. It requires the three-part structure from `narrative-arc.md §12.3`: **constraint → choice → cost**. The current text provides none of these. It provides zero named trade-offs, zero comparison to alternatives, zero articulation of what was rejected and why. ### Problem 2 — Section Hierarchy Inversion (CAP-006 + CFB-019) **Anti-pattern: CAP-006 — Burying the Lede; feedback precedent: CFB-019** "Token Architecture" is subordinated under "Lumen Color Identity" — a subsystem (architecture) is nested under an output (the color palette). This is architecturally backwards: the naming architecture is the *decision layer* that produced Lumen; Lumen is one *application* of that architecture. Nesting the parent under its own child signals to the reader that the color palette is the insight and the architecture is supporting documentation. The correct reading is the inverse: the architecture is the insight, the color palette is evidence it works. CFB-019 caught this exact inversion in an earlier version: *"The information architecture here is just wrong — architecture first, rationale second."* The v3 rebuild appears to have reintroduced it. ### Problem 3 — No Competitive Differentiation (CAP-016 compound) IBM Carbon and Goldman Sachs OneGS are the canonical reference systems any hiring manager at a large design org will know. The current text neither invokes them nor positions against them. This is a missed opportunity for the Reframe beat (Beat 3) and the Decisions beat (Beat 5). The interesting argument isn't "I built a design system" — it's "I built a design system optimized for a constraint Carbon and OneGS don't have: an AI agent that reads your tokens literally and fails silently on ambiguous names." --- ## Fix ### Step 1 — Invert the section hierarchy in the CMS The correct parent-child order: ``` BEFORE (wrong): └── Lumen Color Identity └── Token Architecture ← child of its own output AFTER (correct): └── Token Architecture ← the decision, the WHY └── Lumen Color Identity ← one application proving the architecture works ``` In Payload CMS, the feature sections are ordered content blocks. Move the "Token Architecture" section **above** "Lumen Color Identity" in the drag-and-drop block order in the admin panel at `/admin/collections/projects/[elan-slug]`. ### Step 2 — Rewrite the "Token Architecture" section body using the Decisions beat template Replace the current feature-list body with a trade-off narrative. Template from `narrative-arc.md §12.3` (constraint → choice → cost): **Current (feature-list, CAP-016):** > *"Élan uses a semantic token naming convention: `color/role/prominence`. Tokens are organized by function rather than value, making them readable across components."* **Replacement (Decisions beat, with competitive differentiation):** > Carbon's role-based scheme — `$ui-01`, `$ui-02` — encodes visual hierarchy numerically. It's legible to a trained designer who has memorized the tier map. An AI agent reading `$ui-02` cold has no idea whether it's a background, a border, or a disabled state; the number carries no semantic content. I tried a three-tier path structure first (`color/surface/primary/default`) and the agent reliably confused depth for specificity — it would select `color/surface/primary` when it meant `color/surface/primary/default`, producing silent failures. The flat semantic scheme (`color/surface-primary`) sacrifices tier granularity in exchange for zero-ambiguity resolution. The cost is real: you cannot encode prominence variants without a naming suffix convention. The benefit is that the agent gets the token right on the first attempt, session after session. This body is ~100 words, covers one trade-off with full constraint/choice/cost, names Carbon explicitly, and grounds the decision in actual observed agent behavior — which is the strongest evidence the project has. ### Step 3 — Rewrite the "Lumen Color Identity" section to position it as *evidence*, not the main event The Lumen section should open by explicitly framing itself as a downstream artifact of the architecture: **Current (feature-showcase framing):** > *"Lumen is Élan's color system. It includes a base palette, semantic role tokens, and extended tints for interactive states."* **Replacement (evidence framing):** > Lumen is the first stress test. Every color token in the system was named under the flat semantic scheme — if the naming architecture holds under the full surface area of a color system, it holds. The answer: 40+ tokens, zero agent disambiguation failures after the first three sessions. The semantic names also doubled as documentation — `color/interactive/hover` communicates intent to both an agent and a human reviewer without a reference lookup. ### Step 4 — Add the IBM Carbon / Goldman Sachs comparison to the scope statement or blurb body The comparison belongs in the **Reframe beat** (scope statement or intro blurb body), not buried in the middle of a feature section. The scope statement is where the thesis becomes visible. **Add this sentence to the scope statement** (after the existing constraint framing, before the metric): > Industry benchmarks — IBM Carbon, Goldman Sachs OneGS — are built for humans who can internalize a naming convention over time. Élan is built for a collaborator who reads every token cold, every session. That's a different design problem. This single addition gives the hiring manager the comparison frame before they read any section, which means every subsequent section is read as an argument, not a list. ### Step 5 — Update `docs/content/projects/elan-design-system.md` dossier Add a new frustration log entry and update the Frustration Log and Style Preferences sections: ```markdown ## Frustration Log (additions) - 2026-[date]: "Reads like a feature list — no WHY, no comparison to Carbon or GS, section hierarchy inverted (Token Architecture under Lumen)" — Fixed per CFB-043. NOTE: This is a regression from CFB-014 and CFB-019. The v3 rebuild reintroduced all three issues. Promotion criterion: if the feature-list pattern appears in a third rebuild, escalate to a hard architectural constraint — Token Architecture must always be the parent section, never a child of any color/component section. ``` And add to Style Preferences: ```markdown - **Section hierarchy invariant:** Token Architecture is ALWAYS a parent section, never subordinated to Lumen or any color/component section. Lumen is evidence that the architecture works; it is not the headline. (CFB-014, CFB-019, CFB-043 — three occurrences = escalation threshold met.) - **Competitive framing is mandatory:** IBM Carbon and Goldman Sachs OneGS must be named in the scope statement or the Token Architecture section body. The argument only has stakes if a reader can see what alternative was rejected. ``` ### Step 6 — Log CFB-043 in `docs/content-feedback-log.md` ```markdown ## Session: 2026-[date] — Élan content strategy: feature-list regression, hierarchy re-inversion #### CFB-043: "Reads like a feature list — no comparison to Carbon or GS, Token Architecture under Lumen Color Identity, showcase not rationale" **Intent:** Three compounding failures: (1) token naming convention shown as WHAT without WHY (CAP-016); (2) section hierarchy inverted — Token Architecture subordinated under Lumen Color Identity (CAP-006, CFB-019); (3) no competitive positioning vs IBM Carbon or Goldman Sachs OneGS. **Root Cause:** CAP-016 + CAP-006 regression from v3 rebuild. CFB-014 and CFB-019 resolved these issues in earlier versions; the v3 rebuild (2026-04-06) reintroduced them because the rebuild focused on narrative shape (Translation Arc) and thesis (co-creation) without re-verifying the Decisions beat or section hierarchy constraints. **Resolution:** (1) Token Architecture promoted to parent section; Lumen Color Identity reframed as evidence/downstream artifact. (2) Token Architecture body rewritten as a Decisions beat (constraint/choice/cost) naming Carbon's numerical scheme as the rejected alternative. (3) IBM Carbon + Goldman Sachs comparison added to scope statement as Reframe beat. (4) Dossier updated with regression note and section hierarchy invariant. Three occurrences of hierarchy inversion (CFB-014, CFB-019, CFB-043) meet the escalation threshold — promoted to hard style preference. **Anti-patterns active:** CAP-016 (feature list), CAP-006 (burying the lede / hierarchy) **Escalation:** Section hierarchy invariant added to dossier Style Preferences (3-occurrence threshold). ``` --- ## Summary of Changes | File | Change | |---|---| | Payload CMS → Élan project blocks | Move "Token Architecture" block above "Lumen Color Identity" block | | Payload CMS → Élan scope statement | Add IBM Carbon / Goldman Sachs comparison sentence as Reframe beat | | Payload CMS → Token Architecture body | Rewrite from feature-list to Decisions beat (constraint/choice/cost, names Carbon explicitly) | | Payload CMS → Lumen Color Identity body | Reframe opening sentence as "evidence" framing, not "feature" framing | | `docs/content/projects/elan-design-system.md` | Add frustration log entry + section hierarchy invariant to Style Preferences | | `docs/content-feedback-log.md` | Log CFB-043 as new entry | --- ## Principle **CAP-016 (Feature-List Case Study) is the primary violation.** The corrective structure is always Beat 5 (Decisions) from the Narrative Arc: every design convention shown in a case study must be accompanied by its **constraint** (what forced the decision), its **choice** (specifically what was done and what was rejected), and its **cost** (what was given up and why it was acceptable). A token naming convention without a rejected alternative is a catalog entry. A token naming convention that explains why Carbon's scheme fails under AI agent conditions is a judgment call — which is what a hiring manager is actually evaluating. Two secondary violations compound it: **CAP-006** (hierarchy inversion buries the architecture under its own output) and the absence of competitive reference (IBM Carbon / Goldman Sachs), which strips the Reframe beat of its stakes. All three are fixable with targeted rewrites — no structural rebuild required. This feedback is a **three-occurrence escalation** for the hierarchy inversion specifically (CFB-014 → CFB-019 → current). Per the escalation protocol, it must be promoted to a hard style preference in the dossier, not treated as a fresh correction.
